summary: Chang Yen Mow was the head of the new Department of Mines of the new Chinese Government around 1899. He was looking for a foreign expert who was knowledgeable and honest to be the Director-General of Mines. Acting on a recommendation from an English mining group, he offered the position to Herbert Hoover, which Hoover accepted. Chang was instrumental in initiating the modernization of China's mining sector.

The new Chinese Government around 1899 established a Department of Mines to develop the country's mineral resources. They appointed Chang Yen Mow as the head and sought foreign expertise to modernize mining operations. This government aimed to create a mining code that would allow private enterprise to participate in the industry, moving away from the traditional crown property system. It was a period of reform and opening to foreign knowledge.

At just twenty-four years old in 1899, I was recommended by an English mining group to be the Director-General of Mines for the Chinese Government. Before heading to China, I stopped in London to learn about what would be expected of me. I married Lou Henry in Monterey, California, and shortly after, we sailed from San Francisco to China. My work involved surveying mineral resources and helping frame mining laws for the empire.

Before going to China in early 1899, I spent about two weeks in London. I reported to my employers about their affairs and sought information about the new job as Director-General of Mines for China. London was a key stop on my journey from West Australia to China, helping me prepare for the challenges ahead.

I was working successfully in West Australia as a mining engineer before being offered the position in China. It was from there that I received the cable offer to become Director-General of Mines for the Chinese Government. West Australia was where I had built my early career and reputation in mining.

During my youth, I managed to get into Stanford University by quickly learning enough physiology in just twenty-four hours. This showed my ability to rapidly acquire knowledge when needed. Stanford was an important step in my education before embarking on my mining career.

I arrived in San Francisco on February 1, 1899, before heading to China. It was from here that Lou Henry and I sailed for the Chinese Empire. San Francisco was a key departure point on our journey to my new role as Director-General of Mines.

Early in my career, I took a weekend to learn typewriting so I could work in Louis Janin's office in San Francisco. This was part of my preparation and experience before moving on to more challenging roles. Janin's office was an important early workplace for me.

Monterey was the charming old Pacific capital where I spent a few days in early February 1899. It was here that I married Lou Henry on February 10th. The town was historic and sleepy, and our quick marriage was quite a surprise to the locals, who were used to more traditional celebrations.

Lou Henry, the daughter of a respected town banker, was the woman I married in Monterey on February 10, 1899. She accompanied me to China where she lived as the wife of the Director-General of Mines of the Celestial Empire. Our marriage was swift but marked the beginning of a lifelong partnership.

In 1899, I traveled to China to take up the post of Director-General of Mines under the new Chinese Government. My work involved surveying mineral resources and helping to draft mining laws to encourage private enterprise. China was a land with ancient mining traditions, but it was entering a new era of modernization and reform.

On the month-long voyage from San Francisco to China in 1899, Lou Henry and I sat at table with Frederick Palmer, the war correspondent, and his wife. This was the start of a friendship that has lasted over the years. Traveling with such notable companions made the journey more interesting and memorable.

During our journey to China, we traveled with a secretary of the U.S. legation at Peking and his wife. Peking was the political center where I would eventually report and work as Director-General of Mines. The city was a key location in the Chinese Empire at the time.

Among our shipmates on the way to China were a missionary couple who may or may not have survived the Boxer Rebellion massacres. This event was a significant and violent uprising that affected foreigners and missionaries in China. It was a reminder of the turbulent times in which we were entering.
